items.prompt_md: What question is Big-O actually answering about a piece of code?
options: How many wall-clock seconds it takes to run on your laptop
How the amount of work grows as the input size grows
How many lines of code the solution has
How much disk space the source file takes up
items.answer: How the amount of work grows as the input size grows
items.explanation_md: Big-O describes the *shape* of the growth curve as `n` increases, not a literal timing measurement.

items.prompt_md: A function walks through a list of `n` items once, and for each item does a single hash-set membership check (`item in seen`). What's the overall time complexity?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: One pass of `n` steps, each doing O(1) work (a hash lookup), stays linear overall — O(1) work per element doesn't turn a single loop into something worse.

options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: The inner loop re-scans roughly the rest of the same `n`-element list for every outer-loop step, so the comparisons multiply out to `n * n`.

items.prompt_md: When you simplify a growth expression like `3n^2 + 50n + 100` down to Big-O notation, what do you do with the `50n + 100` part and the `3`?
options: Keep every term because they all matter equally at any `n`
Drop the constants and lower-order terms, keeping only the fastest-growing term
Add them together into a single new exponent
Replace the whole expression with `n` regardless of the leading term
items.answer: Drop the constants and lower-order terms, keeping only the fastest-growing term
items.explanation_md: As `n` grows large, the fastest-growing term dominates the total, so Big-O keeps only that term and discards constants and smaller terms.

items.prompt_md: Which of these lists the common growth classes correctly ordered from slowest-growing to fastest-growing?
options: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n)
O(1) < O(n) < O(log n) < O(n^2) < O(n log n) < O(2^n)
O(2^n) < O(n^2) < O(n log n) < O(n) < O(log n) < O(1)
O(log n) < O(1) < O(n log n) < O(n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n)
items.answer: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n)
items.explanation_md: Constant work grows slowest, then halving-style logarithmic work, then a single pass, then sort-plus-pass, then nested loops, then work that doubles with every added element.

items.prompt_md: A solution first sorts an `n`-element array (O(n log n)) and then does one linear scan over the sorted result (O(n)) to find an answer. What's the overall time complexity of the combined approach?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: When two steps run one after another, the overall complexity is the faster-growing of the two — the O(n log n) sort dominates the O(n) scan that follows it.

items.prompt_md: Looking at code (not running it), what's the tell that an approach is O(n^2) rather than O(n)?
options: It has a single loop over the input
It has a loop nested inside another loop, both ranging over roughly the same `n` elements
It calls a function that does a dict lookup
It uses recursion instead of a loop
items.answer: It has a loop nested inside another loop, both ranging over roughly the same `n` elements
items.explanation_md: Nesting is the visual cue: for each of the `n` outer steps, the inner loop does up to `n` more steps, multiplying out to roughly `n * n` total work.

items.prompt_md: A problem allows `n` up to 10^5, and the only approach you can think of is O(n^2). Judging by the constraints alone, is that approach likely fast enough?
options: Yes, O(n^2) is always fine no matter how large `n` gets
No — at n = 10^5, O(n^2) is on the order of 10^10 operations, almost certainly too slow; look for O(n) or O(n log n)
It depends only on how fast the machine's clock speed is, not on the algorithm
Complexity class stops mattering once `n` exceeds 1,000
items.answer: No — at n = 10^5, O(n^2) is on the order of 10^10 operations, almost certainly too slow; look for O(n) or O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: Reading constraints against the growth ladder is exactly how you sanity-check an approach before writing it: 10^10 operations is far beyond what typically finishes in time, so a linear or linearithmic approach should be the target.

items.prompt_md: An approach repeatedly cuts the remaining search space in half — checking the middle, then discarding one half — until a single element is left. What's the time complexity in terms of `n`?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: Halving the space on every step means the number of steps grows logarithmically with `n` — the classic binary-search shape.

items.prompt_md: A nested-loop pair search can sometimes return early the moment it finds a match, so it doesn't always scan every element. When people say "this approach is O(n^2)", which case are they normally describing?
options: The best case, where a match happens to be found immediately
The worst case — the upper bound on how the work can grow, even if it sometimes finishes sooner
The average number of seconds the program runs for
The case where `n` equals exactly 1
items.answer: The worst case — the upper bound on how the work can grow, even if it sometimes finishes sooner
items.explanation_md: Big-O labels like O(n^2) usually describe the worst case unless stated otherwise — the bound that holds even when no early exit happens.

items.prompt_md: The brute-force pair search uses no extra data structure, while a hash-map version trades that for a dict that can grow to size `n`. What is this second dimension of cost — separate from time — called?
options: Time complexity
Space complexity
Cyclomatic complexity
Amortized complexity
items.explanation_md: Space complexity measures how the extra memory an approach needs grows with the input, independent of how its running time grows.

items.prompt_md: Someone claims: "My loop only runs `n` times, so it's O(n)" — but inside that loop they call a helper that itself scans the whole input. Why is this reasoning a common misconception?
options: It isn't a misconception — a loop that runs `n` times is always O(n) no matter what's inside it
The label ignored the per-iteration cost: an O(n) helper called inside an `n`-iteration loop makes the whole thing O(n^2), not O(n)
It's a misconception because loops are always O(1) regardless of how many times they run
Space complexity, not time complexity, is what determines the loop's growth rate here
items.answer: The label ignored the per-iteration cost: an O(n) helper called inside an `n`-iteration loop makes the whole thing O(n^2), not O(n)
items.explanation_md: Counting operations means counting the work *inside* each iteration too — an O(n) loop that does O(n) work per step is really O(n) * O(n) = O(n^2), a trap that's easy to miss if you only look at the outer loop.

items.prompt_md:
```python
def sum_first_and_last(nums):
    return nums[0] + nums[-1]
```
Assuming index access on a list is O(1), what is the time complexity of `sum_first_and_last`, regardless of how large `nums` is?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: The function does a fixed, constant number of operations — two index lookups and an addition — no matter how many elements `nums` has.

items.prompt_md: Before writing any code for a new problem, the lesson suggests counting operations "out loud" first. What's the main benefit of doing that Big-O check *before* committing to an approach?
options: It guarantees the code will have zero bugs
It lets you catch a bad growth shape (like a hidden nested loop) early and consider a faster approach — hash map, sorting, or a smarter pass — before investing time in coding it
It replaces the need for testing the solution afterward
It tells you the exact runtime in seconds ahead of time
items.answer: It lets you catch a bad growth shape (like a hidden nested loop) early and consider a faster approach — hash map, sorting, or a smarter pass — before investing time in coding it
items.explanation_md: Big-O is meant to be a design-time question, not a post-mortem — spotting the nested-loop tell before coding gives you the chance to reach for a hash map or sort instead.
